schools.summary: Paul solemnly affirms his truthfulness — in Christ he does not lie, his conscience bearing witness in the Holy Spirit (Barnes, Robertson) — a strong protestation guarding against any charge of insincerity in the grief he is about to express for Israel.
Chrysostom (with Calvin): Paul, about to express deep grief for the Jews who opposed him, guards against the charge of insincerity by this solemn affirmation, that they might know his love.

themes: I say the truth in Christ, I lie not
my conscience bearing me witness in the Holy Ghost
Paul's solemn protestation
sincere grief for Israel

schools.summary: Paul confesses great heaviness (grief, pain) and continual sorrow (unceasing anguish) in his heart (Barnes, Robertson) — a deep, abiding grief for unbelieving Israel, his kinsmen cut off by their rejection of the Messiah.
Calvin (with Theodoret): Paul's grief proves his unfeigned affection for his nation, even for those who persecuted him — sorrowing for their lostness.

themes: great heaviness and continual sorrow in my heart
Paul's great sorrow for Israel
deep grief for his lost people
love even for his persecutors

schools.summary: Paul could (almost) wish himself accursed (anathema, devoted to destruction, separated from Christ) for his brethren — the strongest expression of self-sacrificing love; not a fixed resolve (final separation being impossible and unlawful) but the language of overflowing love, a wish he checks, like Moses (Exodus 32:32, Barnes, Robertson, Clarke).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the parallel with Moses — such love seeks the good of others above its own, willing even the greatest loss for their salvation, yet governed by submission to God's will.

themes: I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ
for my brethren, my kinsmen according to the flesh
Paul's astonishing wish of love
self-sacrificing love like Moses

schools.summary: The 'Israelites' (the covenant name, marking descent from Jacob) possessed rich privileges — the adoption (Israel God's son), the glory (the Shekinah presence), the covenants, the giving of the law (at Sinai), the service of God (the temple worship), and the promises (of the Messiah, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): Paul recounts these privileges to magnify the grief of their unbelief — that a nation so favored should reject the Messiah.

themes: who are Israelites; to whom pertaineth the adoption, and the glory
the covenants, the giving of the law, the service of God, and the promises
the rich privileges of Israel
the heritage that magnified their unbelief

schools.summary: The crowning privilege: from Israel's fathers, according to the flesh, came Christ — and 'who is over all, God blessed for ever' declares his deity (Barnes, Robertson, the natural reading), 'according to the flesh' implying a higher nature; Ellicott weighs the alternative doxology-to-the-Father (Socinian) reading but leans to the application to Christ as a little the more probable.

Calvin (with Gill, Clarke, Wesley): a clear testimony to Christ's divinity — the ascription of praise belongs to none but the true and eternal God; the two natures distinguished yet united, Christ both of the fathers and God blessed forever; the punctuation objections a presumptuous attempt to darken the clearest light.

debates.question: Does 'who is over all, God blessed for ever' refer to Christ or is it a doxology to the Father?

sides.position: It declares the deity of Christ — he who came in the flesh is over all, God blessed forever (the natural reading)
Possibly a doxology to the Father (the Socinian reading), though the application to Christ is more probable

themes: of whom as concerning the flesh Christ came
who is over all, God blessed for ever
the deity of Christ
Christ of the fathers, yet over all

schools.summary: God's word has not failed (despite Israel's rejection), for the crucial distinction holds — not all physically descended from Israel are the true Israel; there is a spiritual Israel within the natural, the children of promise, not merely of the flesh (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): God's promise was never to all the natural seed indiscriminately but to the elect, the children of promise — so the rejection of unbelieving Israel does not nullify the word; the grace of God's covenant was not effectual in all.

themes: the word of God hath not taken none effect
they are not all Israel, which are of Israel
the true Israel is the spiritual seed
the children of promise

schools.summary: Mere physical descent from Abraham does not make one a true child of the promise (Ishmael was Abraham's seed but not the child of promise); for God said 'in Isaac shall thy seed be called' (Genesis 21:12) — the line of promise running through Isaac, not Ishmael, by God's election (Barnes, Calvin).
Cyril (with Calvin): the true seed of Abraham is constituted by God's promise and choice, not by natural generation alone.

themes: not all children, because they are the seed of Abraham
In Isaac shall thy seed be called
the seed of promise, not merely of the flesh
the divine choice through Isaac

schools.summary: Paul interprets the Isaac/Ishmael distinction — mere natural descent (children of the flesh) does not constitute one a child of God; rather, the children of promise (born by virtue of God's promise, as Isaac, the spiritual seed) are counted as the true seed of Abraham (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the true children of God are not the merely natural Israel but those constituted by promise (and, in the gospel, by faith) — the spiritual seed distinguished from the carnal.

themes: the children of the flesh, these are not the children of God
the children of the promise are counted for the seed
the children of promise, not of the flesh
the spiritual seed

schools.summary: Paul quotes God's promise (Genesis 18:10, 14) — at the appointed time God would come and Sarah would have a son; Isaac born not by the course of nature (Sarah past childbearing) but by the power of God's promise (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Isaac, the child of promise, is the type of all who are God's children by promise and grace, not by the flesh — the seed constituted by divine power.

themes: this is the word of promise
Sarah shall have a son
the word of promise that gave Isaac
the seed by promise and divine power

schools.summary: Paul advances to a case clearer than Isaac/Ishmael — Jacob and Esau, who had the same father (Isaac) and mother (Rebecca) and were conceived at the same time, twins; unlike Ishmael and Isaac, full brothers, so the distinction could rest on nothing but God's choice (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): this case removes every ground of distinction but God's election (or, for some, foreknowledge), the twins being wholly equal in descent.

themes: when Rebecca also had conceived by one, even by our father Isaac
the clearer case of Jacob and Esau
twins of one father and mother
distinction by God's choice alone

schools.summary: The choice between Jacob and Esau was made before they were born or had done good or evil, that the purpose of God according to election might stand, not of works but of him that calleth (Barnes, Robertson) — so it could not rest on their merits; Barnes affirms sovereign election not of works.

Calvin, Gill: unconditional individual election and reprobation — God chose Jacob and rejected Esau by his free will alone, not from foreseen works; the notion that God elects from foreseen merit is false and contrary to God's word, for he could see nothing in the corrupt nature of either to induce his favor.
Clarke: the election is national (to outward privilege, Israel over Edom), not personal eternal salvation or reprobation — the words spoken of nations, not individuals' eternal states.

debates.question: Is the election here unconditional individual election or national election to privilege?

sides.position: Unconditional individual election (and reprobation) — God's free choice, not from foreseen works
National election to outward privilege (Israel over Edom), not personal eternal salvation
Sovereign election not of works, the reasons undisclosed, without affirming unconditional reprobation

themes: the children being not yet born, neither having done any good or evil
the purpose of God according to election might stand, not of works
the purpose of God according to election
the central crux of election

schools.summary: God's word to Rebecca (Genesis 25:23) reversed the natural order — the elder (Esau) should serve the younger (Jacob) — referring primarily to the posterity: Edom subject to Israel, fulfilled in the nations (Jacob never personally ruled Esau, Barnes).
Calvin: the temporal dominion symbolized something greater — the deeper election; the primogeniture the sign of God's spiritual choice of Jacob.
Clarke (with Wesley): the words are national and temporal — Esau served Jacob only in his posterity (the Edomites under Israel), not personal eternal reprobation.

themes: The elder shall serve the younger
the word to Rebecca
the divine choice reversing the natural order
national, temporal, or symbolic

schools.summary: From Malachi 1:2-3, confirming the choice — God preferred Jacob (and Israel) and rejected Esau (and Edom); 'hated' in the comparative sense, not positive hatred but less loved or favour withheld (the Hebrew idiom, Genesis 29:30-31; Luke 14:26, Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Gill): confirms election and reprobation — God's free love to Jacob and rejection of Esau, led by his mercy alone and no worthiness of works; where God's love is, there is life, where his wrath, death.

Wesley (with Clarke): the words relate not to the persons of Jacob and Esau, nor to their eternal states, but to their posterities (Israel and Edom) — Malachi speaks of the nations' temporal fortunes, not individual damnation.

debates.question: Does 'Jacob loved, Esau hated' refer to individuals' eternal states or to the nations?

sides.position: God's sovereign electing love and reprobation
A national distinction (Israel and Edom) in temporal fortunes, not personal eternal states

themes: Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated
the confirming word from Malachi
love and comparative hatred
the heart of the election controversy

schools.summary: Paul anticipates the natural objection — if God chooses sovereignly, is he not unjust? (the standing objection to the doctrine of sovereignty) — and vehemently rejects it ('God forbid,' me genoito): there is no unrighteousness with God (Barnes, Henry, Robertson).

Calvin: the flesh storms at the doctrine, but Paul does not retreat — he defends God's righteousness; Wesley adds that God's justice is vindicated because he deals with men according to his revealed terms, not arbitrarily.

themes: Is there unrighteousness with God? God forbid
the objection of injustice rejected
sovereignty and justice
the vindication of God's righteousness

schools.summary: Paul quotes God's word to Moses (Exodus 33:19) — God claims the absolute right to bestow mercy and compassion on whom he will; mercy being by nature free and undeserved, owed to none, bestowed according to God's own will, not man's merit (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the absolute sovereignty of electing grace — God shows mercy on whom he pleases; Wesley reads it rather as God's freedom to fix the terms of mercy (mercy to those who believe).

themes: I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy
God's sovereign prerogative of mercy
the freedom of divine grace
mercy on whom he will

schools.summary: Mercy (salvation) does not depend on man's will (desire) nor his running (effort) but on God who shows mercy — excluding human merit as the ground; the whole depending on God's free mercy, the ground of salvation in God alone (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: election and salvation depend wholly on God's mercy, not human will or effort; Wesley reads it as salvation not by man's will or works as meritorious, but by God's mercy received by faith.

themes: not of him that willeth, nor of him that runneth, but of God that sheweth mercy
salvation of God's mercy, not man's will or works
the dependence on divine mercy
the exclusion of human merit

schools.summary: God's word to Pharaoh (Exodus 9:16) — 'raised thee up' means brought onto history's stage and made the occasion for displaying God's power, that God might show his power (in the plagues and Exodus) and spread his name throughout the earth; Pharaoh's obstinacy made the occasion of God's glory (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God ordained Pharaoh for this end; Wesley and Clarke hold God raised up the already-wicked Pharaoh and used his self-hardening to display his power, not decreeing his sin.

themes: Even for this same purpose have I raised thee up
that I might shew my power in thee
Pharaoh raised up to show God's power
God's name declared throughout the earth

schools.summary: God shows mercy on whom he will and hardens whom he will; the hardening is judicial and permissive — God exerts no positive influence to make men sinful but leaves the sinner to his own course, withdrawing restraint (Pharaoh hardened his own heart too, Exodus 8:15, 32; a giving-up as in 1:24, Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: more than mere permission — the operation of God's wrath; God ordains and uses even Satan and external means to blind the reprobate, the ruin of the wicked not only foreseen but ordained by his counsel, yet God is not the author of their sin.

Wesley: he hardeneth — that is, leaves to the hardness of their hearts, in consequence of their obstinate wickedness; God shows mercy on his own terms, to those who believe, and hardens only the obstinate.

debates.question: Is God's hardening a positive act or a judicial withdrawal of restraint?

sides.position: Judicial/permissive — God leaves the sinner to his own course and withdraws restraint (Pharaoh hardened his own heart)
More than permission — the active operation of God's wrath upon the reprobate, yet God not the author of sin

themes: mercy on whom he will have mercy, and whom he will he hardeneth
the two-fold conclusion of mercy and hardening
the hardening crux
sovereign mercy and judgment

schools.summary: The objector argues that if God hardens whom he will and his will cannot be resisted, why does he still find fault? (the standing objection that sovereignty destroys responsibility) — assuming none can successfully oppose God's will, so men cannot be blamed (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the character of the objection — the flesh storming against God, charging him with tyranny; the 'still' and 'his' expressing the objector's surly, murmuring aversion to God.

themes: Why doth he yet find fault? For who hath resisted his will
the objection from sovereignty
sovereignty and responsibility
the human revolt against divine sovereignty

schools.summary: Paul rebukes the presumption rather than solving the difficulty — who is man (a finite, sinful creature) to reply against (dispute with, arraign) his Maker? — quoting Isaiah 29:16; 45:9 on the absurdity of the thing formed reproaching its former, the creature having no standing to question the Creator (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: the appeal is to humility — God refrains from giving reasons because we cannot contain his immense wisdom; we must reverently adore the mystery, not arraign him, the will of God being the rule of all righteousness.

themes: who art thou that repliest against God
Shall the thing formed say to him that formed it, Why hast thou made me thus
the rebuke of the creature's presumption
reverence before the Creator

schools.summary: As the potter has the right to make from the same lump vessels for honour and dishonour, so God has the sovereign right over men (Isaiah 64:8; Jeremiah 18:6); the 'lump' is humanity not merely as created but as fallen — the condemned mass — so God, dealing with sinners who have no claim, does injustice to none (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke).

Calvin: God's absolute right to appoint the lot of his creatures — the potter takes nothing from the clay; God is deprived of his honour unless such authority over men, as arbiter of life and death, be conceded to him; the lump the condemned mass of fallen humanity.

Wesley: the potter-figure shows God's right as Creator and Proprietor (dispensing gifts by rules out of our sight), but as moral Governor he deals with men according to their works — appointing the believer to honour, the unbeliever to dishonour; not an arbitrary or tyrannical will.

debates.question: Does the potter-figure teach God's absolute sovereignty over each individual's eternal lot, or his right as Creator joined with just dealing by works?

sides.position: God's absolute sovereign right to appoint the lot of his creatures
God's right as Creator, but as moral Governor he deals with men according to their works (believer to honour, unbeliever to dishonour)

themes: Hath not the potter power over the clay
one vessel unto honour, and another unto dishonour
the potter's sovereign right over the clay
God's sovereignty over fallen humanity

schools.summary: God, purposing to show his wrath and power, endured with much longsuffering the vessels of wrath 'fitted' (katertismena) to destruction — and Paul deliberately does not say God fitted them (unlike the vessels of mercy he 'afore prepared,' v.23); the agency is left unnamed, they being self-fitted by sin or ripe for ruin (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: both the reprobate and the elect are prepared by God's secret counsel — the vessels of wrath destined to destruction before they are born; God's longsuffering serving to magnify his wrath and power, though the proximate cause of their ruin is their own sin.

Wesley: God endured the vessels of wrath with much longsuffering — they fitted themselves for destruction by their obstinate wickedness; the change of language (fitted, not prepared) shows God is not the author of their ruin.

debates.question: Are the vessels of wrath 'fitted to destruction' by themselves or by God?

sides.position: Self-fitted by their own sin — Paul deliberately avoids naming God as the agent (contrast 'afore prepared' in v.23)
Prepared by God's secret counsel, the reprobate destined to destruction

themes: endured with much longsuffering the vessels of wrath fitted to destruction
God's longsuffering with the vessels of wrath
the 'fitted' crux
the asymmetry of wrath and mercy

schools.summary: God's purpose is to make known the riches of his glory on the vessels of mercy, which he himself 'afore prepared' (proetoimasen) unto glory — the key difference from v.22, where God is not named as fitting the vessels of wrath; the renewing of the elect God's own work of grace (Ephesians 2:10, Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: God prepares the elect by his secret counsel and electing grace — the vessels of mercy delivered from the same gulf of destruction by his gratuitous kindness, that his infinite mercy might appear; Wesley reads it as God preparing by grace those who would be vessels of mercy.

themes: the riches of his glory on the vessels of mercy
which he had afore prepared unto glory
the vessels of mercy prepared by God
the asymmetry magnifying grace

schools.summary: Paul identifies the vessels of mercy as believers — 'us,' whom God has called (effectually, by grace) — drawn from both Jews and Gentiles, the gospel breaking the national barrier, the vessels of mercy not confined to Israel but including the believing Gentiles (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret (with Calvin): God's electing mercy extends to all nations, calling a people from both Jews and Gentiles — the great theme of the gospel's universality.

themes: even us, whom he hath called
not of the Jews only, but also of the Gentiles
the vessels of mercy from Jews and Gentiles
the universal reach of electing grace

schools.summary: Paul quotes Hosea (2:23; 1:10) — God would call 'my people' those who were not his people, and 'beloved' her who was not beloved — applying the words (originally of restored Israel) to the calling of the Gentiles, those once not God's people now made his by grace (Barnes, Henry, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the prophets foretold that God would gather a people from those outside the covenant — the Gentiles — fulfilling his purpose of mercy.

themes: I will call them my people, which were not my people
her beloved, which was not beloved
the calling of the Gentiles foretold in Hosea
the prophetic ground of the Gentiles' inclusion

schools.summary: Hosea (1:10) — in the very place where they were called 'not my people,' there they shall be called the children of the living God, a complete reversal of status; those once outside not merely called God's people but his children, adopted into his family (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the Gentiles, once aliens, are by grace made children of the living God — the prophecy fulfilled in the gospel's gathering of the nations.

themes: where it was said, Ye are not my people; there shall they be called the children of the living God
the outsiders made children of God
the complete reversal of status
adoption of the Gentiles

schools.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah (10:22) — concerning Israel, though their number be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant shall be saved; despite Israel's vast numbers, only a small remaining part would be saved, the mass rejecting the Messiah but a believing remnant preserved (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): the prophets foretold that not all Israel but only a remnant would be saved — confirming that the rejection of the unbelieving mass was according to Scripture, and that God's word had not failed (v.6).

themes: though the number of the children of Israel be as the sand of the sea, a remnant shall be saved
Isaiah's prophecy of the saved remnant
only a part of Israel saved
God's word not failed

schools.summary: God would finish the work of judgment and cut it short in righteousness, executing his decree swiftly and decisively upon the land (Isaiah 10:23); the judgment brief and conclusive, God bringing his purpose quickly to pass (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the judgment, though severe, is just — God executes it in righteousness, sparing the remnant.

themes: he will finish the work, and cut it short in righteousness
a short work will the Lord make upon the earth
the swift and righteous judgment
God's decisive purpose

schools.summary: But for God's leaving a seed (a remnant, Isaiah 1:9), Israel would have been utterly destroyed as Sodom and Gomorrha — completely consumed, leaving none (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): it is by God's free grace alone that even a remnant is preserved — the survival of any owing not to Israel's merit but to the Lord's mercy; the remnant a monument of grace.

themes: Except the Lord of Sabaoth had left us a seed
we had been as Sodoma, and been made like unto Gomorrha
the preserved seed of grace
mercy alone saving from total ruin

schools.summary: The striking paradox — the Gentiles, who were not pursuing righteousness (not seeking justification, living in sin), nevertheless attained it, the righteousness which is of faith — not by works but by faith, embracing the gospel and receiving the righteousness of God freely (Barnes, Henry, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): the Gentiles, not seeking it, found righteousness by God's free grace through faith — preventing grace magnified, God found of those who sought him not (Isaiah 65:1); faith itself a portion of his favor.

themes: the Gentiles, which followed not after righteousness, have attained to righteousness
the righteousness which is of faith
the Gentiles attaining righteousness by faith
the paradox of grace

schools.summary: Israel zealously pursued the law of righteousness — striving to attain justification by keeping the law — yet did not attain it, failing to reach justification because the law demands perfect obedience which none can render (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): Israel sought righteousness by works not faith, and so failed — the very zeal for legal righteousness causing them to fall short of the true righteousness of faith.

themes: Israel, which followed after the law of righteousness, hath not attained
Israel's failure despite their pursuit
the futility of seeking righteousness by the law
works cannot justify

schools.summary: The decisive reason for Israel's failure — they sought righteousness by works (their own law-keeping) rather than by faith, depending on their own merit, not God's mercy — and so stumbled at the stumbling-stone, which is Christ, being offended at the crucified Messiah (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: trust in works is the chief hindrance, closing the way to righteousness — whoever obtrudes his own righteousness robs Christ of his office; Wesley adds it was not God's decree but their seeking by works that caused their fall.

themes: they sought it not by faith, but as it were by the works of the law
they stumbled at that stumblingstone
the reason for Israel's failure
works not faith, stumbling at Christ

schools.summary: Paul combines Isaiah 8:14 and 28:16 — God lays in Zion a stone which is both a stumbling-stone and rock of offence (to the unbelieving) and a precious, sure foundation (to believers); Christ a stone of stumbling not in himself but through their unbelief, the same stone the foundation of salvation to those who believe (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): those who believe on Christ shall not be ashamed (disappointed) — their hope of justification sure; the stumbling belongs not to Christ in himself but to the wickedness of men who stumble through their wilful unbelief.

themes: Behold, I lay in Sion a stumblingstone and rock of offence
whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed
Christ the stumbling-stone and sure foundation
the dividing effect of the gospel
